---
title: "Template Names"
description: "Choose stable template names in Go and check whether a name is already taken."
---

# Template Names

Template names are the human-readable identifiers you use to build templates and create sandboxes from them.

## Go-specific behavior

- `Build(...)` and `BuildInBackground(...)` take the template name as a positional `name string`
- `Create(ctx, name, nil)` uses the same name string to start a sandbox from a built template
- `BuildOptions.BasicBuildOptions.Alias` is only a legacy fallback when `name == ""`
- `BuildInfo.Name` and `BuildInfo.Alias` currently mirror the created template name
- `Exists(...)` is the preferred availability check; `AliasExists(...)` is deprecated

## Build a named template

A name is assigned when you build the template. After that, you can use the same name with `Create(...)`.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()
	template := e2b.Template(nil).FromPythonImage("3.12")

	buildInfo, _ := e2b.Build(ctx, template, "my-python-env", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			CpuCount: 2,
			MemoryMB: 2048,
		},
	})
	sandbox, _ := e2b.Create(ctx, "my-python-env", nil)

	if buildInfo != nil {
		_ = buildInfo.Name
		_ = buildInfo.Alias
		_ = buildInfo.TemplateID
		_ = buildInfo.BuildID
	}
	_ = sandbox
}
```

## Team-local naming

Template names are scoped to your team.

- your template named `my-app` is stored as `your-team-slug/my-app`
- within your team, you can usually reference it simply as `my-app`
- other teams can have their own `my-app` without conflict
- public templates should generally be referenced using the namespaced form `team-slug/template-name`

Existing public templates may still be reachable without the team slug, but new public references should prefer the fully namespaced form.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()

	teamLocal, _ := e2b.Create(ctx, "my-app", nil)
	publicTemplate, _ := e2b.Create(ctx, "acme/my-app", nil)

	_ = teamLocal
	_ = publicTemplate
}
```

## Common naming patterns

### Development and production environments

Use different names for different environments.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()
	template := e2b.Template(nil).FromBaseImage()

	dev, _ := e2b.Build(ctx, template, "myapp-dev", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			CpuCount: 1,
			MemoryMB: 1024,
		},
	})
	prod, _ := e2b.Build(ctx, template, "myapp-prod", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			CpuCount: 4,
			MemoryMB: 4096,
		},
	})

	_ = dev
	_ = prod
}
```

### Multiple template variants

Create variants of the same template with names that reflect their intended size or behavior.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()
	template := e2b.Template(nil).FromBaseImage()

	small, _ := e2b.Build(ctx, template, "myapp-small", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			CpuCount: 1,
			MemoryMB: 512,
		},
	})
	large, _ := e2b.Build(ctx, template, "myapp-large", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			CpuCount: 8,
			MemoryMB: 8192,
		},
	})

	_ = small
	_ = large
}
```

When the template definition is otherwise the same, E2B can reuse shared build layers across these variants.

## Check name availability

Use `Exists(...)` to check whether a name is already taken in your team.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()

	exists, _ := e2b.Exists(ctx, "my-template", nil)
	_ = exists
}
```

## Best practices

- use descriptive names that match the template's purpose
- use [tags](/docs/template/tags) for versioning instead of embedding versions in the name
- keep naming conventions consistent across your team
- prefer the explicit `name` argument over the legacy `Alias` fallback

For the full template build surface, see the [Go SDK Template reference](/docs/sdk-reference/go-sdk/template).
